author: @MrTalhakamran2006
text:
hummm!
Salam Rob, I played with magnets last year. Then I gave up. I tried the scenario where magnets are tied with cotton string...PROBLEM with cotton string is that after many rotations it will coil up and then will rotate the other way around. The best way to avoid that is to tie that string to a nail and then have the pointing side of nail attached to a metal to which a magnet is attached (to create enough attraction so that nail doesn't fall).

author: @sdavidpringle
text:
Nobody would argue that the moon continues to orbit the Earth because of some energy being added to the system to maintain the motion.  Yet I believe that most people find perpetual motion an impossible fantasy.  To me, it would not surprise me to see something spin apparently forever in a magnetic field.  Perpetual motion.

On Earth, there are too many sources of friction most of the time.

author: @KimberlyRPeacock
text:
What is work? It is the deformation resulting from an energy exchange.  Put electricity in a wire and make electrons move.  The electrons move slowly but the electromagnetic field they generate that is what travels at the speed of light.  And unless you have a superconductor, there is resistance to electron flow, and that generates heat.  Conversely if we heat a metal and attach a different metal and cool it we will generate electricity, and it will also cause those electrons to dance.  There is beauty in the symmetry of nature.  Solar cells a century ago would have been considered perpetual motion, and in the future we may have found a way to create Feynman's demons, but it too won't be perpetual and it won't be an angel of a demon., :)  It will be a chemical oscillator.  There will be an exchange of mass and energy via different potentials, they will each go from high to low, obeying entropy and be out of phase to cycle.  In such as case it is still not perpetual because machines break down, mass leaks, etc.  

One cannot create new energy, but one can conserve internal energy for a long time,  carnot was right in that you cannot convert more work out of an engine than the energy flow through the engine. However one could capture the energy rejected. How?  Simple If you have an ambient temperature  of 10 C and you oscillate between 10C and -5 C but do so at a time rate faster than the diffusion rate, then the external temperature will be the same as ambient, you can lose no thermal energy! Let's assume you have some sort of absorber which absorbs a gas at -5C at half the Pressure that the absorber will release the gas up to at 10C. This is not over unity, as the gas work is a small fraction of the heat energy which must be moved back and forth in and out of the absorber. It's no violation of entropy because when we release the matter from the absorber it undergoes an endothermic reaction and it pressure would quickly drop down if we did not provide heat, but lets say the matter is hydrogen, which is the most energy dense substance but it also has the greatest volume at any given pressure. It s what you want if you want a large deformation from a small bit of matter.  That volume and pressure can run a vapor compression system in which when the absorber/hydride releases hydrogen it compresses the refrigerant and the heat is rejected to the hydride. Therefore the refrigerant becomes the thermal battery storing the energy when it expands, thereby cooling the hydrides, and returning the energy when compressed.  It's not over unity. However it shows that yes you can come close to perpetual motion, just like oxfords bells.  However even those shall wear down over time.

replies.author: @Equinoxtrills
replies.text: I agree, unless in a perfect world everything never breaks down,you could only store energy for some time before it breaks down (although it wouldn't hurt to make it lasts longer say 10.000 years or so hipothetically speaking) me and my friends are working on GO based batteries and it looks promising but no home lab could reproduce the results

author: @KimberlyRPeacock
text:
Build an artificial muscle using fishing line and sewing thread.  Make the line conductive and thin so you can heat it electrically from the magnetic induction.  Threads drive the magnet to spin with its mass and magnets get slowed down, then threads contract and spin the magnets in the other direction.  At first like that famous bridge that came apart as a result of resonance, all you need is the magnets to seek north and south pole, and they should build up enough coupling with the strings to get the job done.

The interesting thing is that I initially dismissed the string entirely, figured they were unimportant, but the fact that we needed the strands and such, I started to think of it as a coupled oscillator.   For the same reason that for a carpenter everything is hammer and nail. I study oscillators and thermodynamics in chemical oscillators.  Oh and it will can stop, but because the magnets are unstable, and are always going to try and align with the poles, all it takes is a little extra jolt to get to a minimum level of actuation of the threaded component and we can dance again.  It should function better in a vacuum. Better if we could suspend it with the string above an inductract such that once we got enough momentum it would lift off an levitate. This would ensure the transfer of energy back and forth from the string was better preserved.    You might improve by coating the fine filaments with graphene enabling quicker heat transfer.  It's an interesting scientific toy.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice one

author: @KimberlyRPeacock
text:
Seeing Ed's video gave me enough of an idea of what is going on.  The problem he is having is a result of earnshaw's theorem, but luckily there are a couple of ways out of stably levitating.  One is rotating, and the coupling is either to local magnetic fields or earth's magnetic field which the magnets try to align with but because its unstable its going to wobble, and if you can put it on a spring (as a way to store some of the energy) you can make it last a long time, even more so if in a vacuum with no air friction.

With the Gentleman who says there is no point in going through this exercise I disagree.  The process of thinking about what is happening, breaking down the problem, thinking about how do we take measurements to better understand, are all useful.  It is the journey not the destination.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: absolutely the point of this video - to state that something is just so - or just not so - is entirely wrong in the spirit of investigation

replies.author: @KimberlyRPeacock
replies.text: Ahh you can eliminate some things, once you have experimented, or thought it out. Then you can test it.  However I think the beauty of this exercise is that its not as simple as a single component, its the interaction of a couple of components as a dynamic system, that makes it fun.

author: @pixelspring
text: I am Prepared to be proven wrong, but could it be as simple as hanging any weight from a piece of cotton.  Cotton is when manufactured a twisted thread and when put in free end tension will want to unwind that torsion.  Try it, any weight will spin, develop rotational inertia in the spinning mass which will be enough to overtwist the thread the opposite way - which will create a torsional spring in that sense and repeat the process until the whole thread has come to its equilibrium untwisted length.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: could well be mate

author: @boxerlego
text: I wouldn't exactly call it perpetual motion but it sure does look fascinating none the less. I built one and strung it up on the ceiling. From a point rest the magnet started to rotate and keep gaining momentum but it only lasted for about a minute or two and once the string got tight enough to turn the magnet under the tension the string had developed. Now once everything balanced out between the magnet the string, the magnet stop rotating. It is just slightly twisting back and fourth in the air. The Interesting thing I want to point out is when I disconnected the magnet from the string you can exactly see that in fact the magnet is holding a equilibrium force between the tension of the string and the magnetic field as the string bunches up under the held tension that is released.

replies.author: @KimberlyRPeacock
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith It is interesting. It reminds me of the tale of the blind engineers who each felt part of the elephant and described it based on that part.  

Clues ,,,,
Fishing line and sewing thread,  bowl shaped magnets which are inherently unstable..  

http://singularityhub.com/2014/03/10/superstrong-artificial-muscles-developed-from-fishing-line-and-sewing-thread/

The above is a part and it acts as both spring and motor, just like hydrides act as thermal compressors, or heat pumps. It's a dual acting system.

The magnets which impart the force is like a low friction pendulum, naturally they are unstable but seek to align.  It's very clever and there are some variation.  Can we induce a current from the magnet spin and send it into the nylon threads, and vice versa when the thread unwinds can we drive a field into the magnets.  

I'm going to have to try that.

author: @actudoran
text:
lol :)) 
as soon as i saw it i took my n50 magnets that i play with and done the experiment ... 
I go cubes of roughly 4 mm a side ... about 50 of them.
Took he cotton string and tied it to my desk lamp and then tried different configurations. if N-S axis is horizontal then it would obviously give me a pointer of earth's N-S alignment ...  
when N-S axis is azimuthal ( don't matter which is up or down ... only change is the proffered direction of spin ) you get mad spins :))  obviously untill the string gathers sufficient torque to fight it ... 
I also experimented in the past with a bogus free energy "motor" which relied upon same effect ... forgot its name .... sorry.
Thing is this works best at 45 degrees of Earth's N-S axis and am pretty sure it won't do a thing at either of the magnetic poles ...
The magnets are trying to line up with the mag field of the Earth ... this effect ... shouldn't happen if the magnetic field of the plant was uniform ... but it's not ... a little torque created ... 

My 2 pennies :)

replies.text:

it relies on the fact that on the sphere here is a slight misalignment ... that's why the metallic ball - being the centre of spin -  rolls about and doesn't keep to its steady position ... if you remove the ball and place say a pin instead ... nothing happens :)
